title: 23 Hidar — St. Solomon the King & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "23 Hidar — St. Solomon the King & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"23 Hidar — St. Solomon the King & The Assembly

narrative\": \"Hidar

St. Solomon the King and Prophet (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Cornelius the Centurion (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Amphilochius of Iconium, Bishop

The twenty-third day of Hidar places the monthly Solomon beside the monthly Cornelius the Centurion — and this pairing, unique to the month in which both receive their simultaneous commemoration, creates one of the calendar's most compelling theological contrasts. Solomon is the king whose wisdom was the greatest the world had known, whose Temple was the supreme architectural achievement of the Israelite civilization, whose prayer at the Temple's dedication asked whether the Most High could truly dwell in a house that human hands had built. His question received its fullest answer not in his own lifetime but in the Entry of Mary into the Temple three months earlier in the calendar year: the Temple that human hands built was always a preparation for the Temple that God would build in the body of a woman, and Solomon's prayer — 'will God indeed dwell on the earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you, how much less this house that I have built' — is answered by the Incarnation with a specificity that exceeds the question's own imagination.

Cornelius the Centurion, commemorated monthly, arrives on the twenty-third day of the apostolic month as the supreme image of the apostolic vesting crossing every boundary that the previous dispensation had maintained. He was a Gentile; he was a Roman military officer; he was the representative of the occupying imperial power; he was everything that the pre-Pentecost community would have identified as standing outside the covenant. And yet the Spirit fell on him before Peter had finished speaking, before the baptismal water had been poured, before the formal structures of the community's reception had been enacted — because the apostolic vesting operates according to the divine initiative, not according to the community's administrative calendar. In the month of the Apostolic Vesting, Cornelius is the testimony that the commission is distributed by the One who sends, and that the boundaries maintained by those who have been sent are not identical with the boundaries maintained by the One who sends.

St. Amphilochius of Iconium was the fourth-century bishop whose friendship with both the Cappadocian Fathers and the Council of Constantinople gave him a position of unusual theological influence in the era of the Nicene settlement. He is remembered particularly for his clarity about the full divinity of the Holy Spirit — a clarity that complemented and extended Athanasius's clarity about the Son. If Athanasius stood against the world for the homoousios of the Son, Amphilochius stood with his generation for the homoousios of the Spirit, completing the Trinitarian theology that the apostolic commission requires as its doctrinal foundation. In the day before the feast of the Twenty-Four Elders, Amphilochius is the theologian who ensures that the heavenly liturgy makes theological sense — who gives the community the vocabulary to understand what the Elders are doing before the Throne.

Solomon, Cornelius, and Amphilochius together on the twenty-third day of Hidar prepare the community for the great feast that arrives tomorrow. Solomon's Temple was the earthly type of the heavenly sanctuary that the Elders inhabit. Cornelius's Spirit-reception before his formal reception demonstrates the divine initiative that underlies all apostolic vesting, including the Elders' eternal appointment. Amphilochius's Trinitarian theology provides the framework within which the heavenly liturgy of the Elders and the earthly liturgy of the martyrs can be understood as participations in the same divine life. The twenty-third day is the preparation of the twenty-fourth: the king, the centurion, and the bishop preparing the ground for the celestial choir.
